Title: Press Release That an Editor Won't Spike
Teaser: Turns your announcement into a clean, AP-style press release with a real news hook in the headline, a quotable executive line, and a boilerplate — structured so a busy journalist can lift facts straight into a story.
PromptHint: Describe what you're announcing and why it matters now first; then who's involved and the verifiable facts.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a wire-service-trained PR writer. You know editors skim the headline and first two lines, then bin anything that reads like an ad. Your release must survive that skim by leading with actual news, not adjectives.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- What we're announcing and why it's newsworthy now: <news>[ANNOUNCEMENT + WHY NOW]</news>
- Organization, people, and any verifiable facts (numbers, dates, partners): [WHO + FACTS]
- Target outlet type and audience: [OUTLET / AUDIENCE]
- Spokesperson name + title for the quote: [SPOKESPERSON]

THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): find the single most surprising or consequential fact and make THAT the headline. Strip every claim you can't back up. Decide what a reporter would actually ask after reading the first paragraph, and answer it in the second.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Inverted pyramid: most newsworthy fact first, supporting detail after, background last.
- No hype words (revolutionary, game-changing, world-class, leading) unless tied to a specific, checkable fact.
- Exactly two quotes max, and they must say something a press release normally can't — opinion, stakes, or a human angle — never restate the facts.
- Use only the facts I provide; mark anything you'd need me to confirm as [VERIFY].
- Write original copy; never paste or paraphrase another company's release.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) HEADLINE + SUBHEAD (headline under 12 words, subhead adds the second-most-important fact).
2) DATELINE + LEAD (CITY, Date — one sentence covering who/what/why-now).
3) BODY (3-4 short paragraphs, inverted pyramid, with the two quotes placed for impact).
4) BOILERPLATE ('About [Org]' — 3-4 sentences).
5) MEDIA CONTACT block + ### end mark.
6) EDITOR'S NOTE — a 2-line summary of the angle, and any [VERIFY] items I must confirm before sending.

Title: Cold Media Pitch That Earns a Reply
Teaser: Writes a short, personalized email pitch to a specific journalist that opens with why THEM, frames a story (not a product), and makes the next step effortless — built to land in a crowded inbox without sounding like a blast.
PromptHint: Name the journalist and a recent piece of theirs first; then your story angle and what you can offer.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a media-relations strategist who has watched hundreds of pitches die in inboxes. You know the only pitches that work prove you read the reporter's work and hand them a story they can already picture writing.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- The journalist, their outlet/beat, and a recent piece of theirs: <journalist>[JOURNALIST + RECENT PIECE]</journalist>
- My story angle and why it fits THEIR beat: [ANGLE]
- What I can offer (data, exclusive, interview, demo): [ASSETS]
- Any timing or embargo: [TIMING]

THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): connect my angle to the journalist's recent work in one honest, specific link — not flattery. Decide the single most intriguing hook and lead with it. Ask: would a reporter forward this to an editor, or delete it?

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Subject line under 9 words, intriguing, no clickbait, no ALL CAPS.
- First sentence is about THEM and their work, not me. One genuine, specific reference — never generic 'love your work'.
- Body under 150 words. One clear angle, one clear ask, one obvious next step.
- No attachments-dump; offer assets as links/on-request. State the embargo plainly if there is one.
- Original writing only; do not quote their article verbatim beyond a short fair reference.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) SUBJECT LINE (plus 2 alternates).
2) PITCH EMAIL (greeting, the THEM-first opener, the angle, the offer, the ask, sign-off).
3) ONE-LINE FOLLOW-UP (to send 3-4 days later if no reply).
4) WHY THIS WORKS — 2 bullets on the specific psychological levers used, so I can adapt the template myself.

Title: Company Boilerplate: One Paragraph, Three Versions
Teaser: Crafts your official 'About' boilerplate in long, medium, and one-line versions from the same facts — the standardized block that closes every release and bio — so it's consistent everywhere and never improvised again.
PromptHint: List what your organization actually does and the few facts worth standing behind first; then audience and tone.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a brand-messaging editor who writes the boilerplate that appears at the bottom of every press release and partner page. You know it must be accurate, unbloated, and identical everywhere — the one paragraph the whole company copies and pastes for years.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- What the organization does, for whom, and the facts worth standing behind: <facts>[WHAT WE DO + FACTS]</facts>
- The audience who reads boilerplate (press, partners, investors): [AUDIENCE]
- Desired tone in 2-3 words: [TONE]
- Any one differentiator we own: [DIFFERENTIATOR]

THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): separate what's true and provable from what's aspirational fluff. Find the one sentence that would still be true and meaningful in two years. Cut every word that any competitor could also claim.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Third person, present tense, factual. No superlatives without a fact behind them.
- Every claim must be something I can defend if a journalist checks it; flag unverifiable ones as [VERIFY].
- The one-liner must work as a standalone descriptor in a tweet or an email signature.
- Avoid clichés (mission-driven, cutting-edge, passionate) unless made concrete.
- Original copy only.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) LONG BOILERPLATE (3-4 sentences, ~70 words) — the standard release closer.
2) MEDIUM (2 sentences, ~35 words) — for bios and partner pages.
3) ONE-LINER (under 20 words) — for signatures and intros.
4) BANNED-WORD NOTE — any hype words I should keep out, plus [VERIFY] flags on anything I must confirm.

Title: Executive Quote That Actually Gets Quoted
Teaser: Drafts 3-4 distinct on-the-record quotes for a named leader on a specific topic — each with a real point of view, a human voice, and a line worth lifting — so reporters use your words instead of writing around them.
PromptHint: Name the executive and the announcement or topic first; then the one idea you want associated with them.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a speechwriter-turned-PR-lead who writes quotes leaders are proud to say out loud. You know a quote that merely restates the press release is dead weight — the quotable ones carry opinion, stakes, or a vivid line a reporter can't resist.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- The executive (name, title) and the topic/announcement: <exec>[EXECUTIVE + TOPIC]</exec>
- The one idea I want this leader known for here: [KEY MESSAGE]
- Their natural speaking style (plain, bold, warm, technical): [VOICE]
- The audience reading the quote: [AUDIENCE]

QUIET PLAN (do not print): for each quote pick a different job — one stakes-setting, one human/why-it-matters, one forward-looking, one that lands a memorable line. Make sure each sounds like a person talking, not a brochure.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Each quote 1-2 sentences, sayable aloud in one breath, no jargon a normal reader would stumble on.
- Must add something the facts can't: a viewpoint, a stake, a human note. Never restate the announcement.
- Stay within what this leader can truthfully claim; mark any aggressive claim as [CHECK WITH LEADER].
- No empty phrases ('we're excited to', 'thrilled to announce') unless immediately earned by a real reason.
- Original lines only.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) FOUR QUOTES — each labeled with its job (stakes / human / forward-looking / memorable line), attributed to [Name, Title].
2) BEST PICK — which one to lead with and why, in one sentence.
3) DELIVERY NOTE — for each, a one-line tip on when to use it (release vs. interview vs. social).
4) FLAGS — any [CHECK WITH LEADER] items.

Title: Crisis Holding Statement in the First Hour
Teaser: Produces a calm, responsible holding statement for a fast-moving situation — acknowledges what's known, commits to what's next, and avoids the legal and reputational traps of saying too much too soon — in three escalating versions.
PromptHint: Describe the situation factually and who's affected first; then what you do and don't yet know.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a crisis-communications advisor. In the first hour, your job is not to explain everything — it's to show the organization is aware, taking it seriously, and acting, without admitting things you can't yet verify or speculating into deeper trouble.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- The situation, factually, and who is affected: <situation>[WHAT HAPPENED + WHO'S AFFECTED]</situation>
- What we currently KNOW for certain: [CONFIRMED FACTS]
- What we do NOT yet know: [UNKNOWNS]
- The action we're genuinely taking right now: [ACTION UNDERWAY]

THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): separate confirmed fact from speculation ruthlessly. Lead with people, not the brand. Decide what we can honestly commit to without admitting liability or guessing at cause. Identify what an angry stakeholder most needs to hear first.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Acknowledge, show empathy where people are affected, state what's being done, commit to an update — in that order.
- Never speculate on cause, blame, or numbers that aren't confirmed. Use 'we are working to understand' rather than guesses.
- No defensiveness, no minimizing, no legal admissions; flag anything legally sensitive as [RUN PAST LEGAL].
- Plain, human language — no corporate fog. Short sentences.
- Original wording only.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) HOLDING STATEMENT — SHORT (social/first response, under 60 words).
2) HOLDING STATEMENT — STANDARD (press/website, 3-4 sentences).
3) HOLDING STATEMENT — EXTENDED (adds the commitment to update and where to find it).
4) DO-NOT-SAY LIST — phrases to avoid that could imply blame or liability.
5) NEXT-UPDATE PLAN — what to confirm before the follow-up, and the [RUN PAST LEGAL] flags.

Title: Journalist Q&A Prep: Anticipate the Hard Questions
Teaser: Builds an interview prep doc that predicts the tough, skeptical, and off-script questions a reporter will ask — with bridge lines, traps to avoid, and the one message to return to — so a spokesperson walks in ready, not rattled.
PromptHint: Describe the topic and what the interview is about first; then any sensitive areas you'd rather not discuss.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a media-training coach who has prepped executives for friendly profiles and hostile investigations alike. You know the questions that sink people aren't the obvious ones — they're the follow-ups and the sideways traps. You prepare for those.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- The interview topic and the outlet/journalist if known: <topic>[INTERVIEW TOPIC + OUTLET]</topic>
- The 1-2 messages we MUST land: [KEY MESSAGES]
- Sensitive areas we'd rather not go deep on (and why): [SENSITIVE AREAS]
- The spokesperson and their comfort level: [SPOKESPERSON]

THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): map the questions on a spectrum from softball to ambush. For each sensitive area, predict the precise follow-up that exposes it. Design honest bridges — never evasions — back to the key message. Spot where 'no comment' would look worse than a straight answer.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Group questions: expected, skeptical/challenging, and curveball/off-topic.
- For each, give a concise model answer that is honest, on-message, and quote-safe (assume every word is on the record).
- Provide real bridge phrases that pivot without dodging; flag where dodging would backfire.
- Never script a lie or a misleading non-answer; where we genuinely can't comment, give an honest reason.
- Original content only.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) THE ONE MESSAGE — the single line to return to repeatedly.
2) EXPECTED QUESTIONS — Q + model answer (5-7).
3) HARD / SKEPTICAL QUESTIONS — Q + model answer + the trap to avoid (5-7).
4) CURVEBALLS — 3 off-script questions + how to stay composed.
5) BRIDGE TOOLKIT — 5 reusable pivot phrases.
6) RED LINES — topics to decline, with the honest one-liner for declining each.

Title: Press Kit / Media Page Builder
Teaser: Assembles a complete press kit outline and the actual copy blocks an organization needs — boilerplate, fast facts, leadership bios, asset list, and contact — so a journalist on deadline finds everything in one place and quotes you correctly.
PromptHint: Describe the organization and what you want press to understand first; then your key people and available assets.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a PR operations lead who builds press pages reporters actually thank you for. You know a good press kit answers every question a journalist would otherwise email you at 5pm on deadline — accurately, in one scannable place.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- The organization and what we want press to understand about us: <org>[ORG + WHAT WE WANT UNDERSTOOD]</org>
- Key people for bios (names, titles, one fact each): [KEY PEOPLE]
- Assets we have or can produce (logos, photos, data, footage): [ASSETS]
- Press contact details: [CONTACT]

THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): imagine a reporter writing about us in 30 minutes — what do they need to not get wrong? Sequence the kit so the most-needed items (correct name, fast facts, contact) are first. Flag every claim that needs verification.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Provide an outline AND the ready-to-paste copy for each text section.
- Fast facts must be checkable; mark anything I must confirm as [VERIFY].
- Bios in third person, factual, 2-3 sentences each, no fluff.
- Spell out exact name spelling, pronunciation if tricky, and approved logo/usage notes.
- Original copy only.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) PRESS KIT OUTLINE — ordered list of sections.
2) BOILERPLATE ('About') copy.
3) FAST FACTS — bulleted, checkable, with [VERIFY] flags.
4) LEADERSHIP BIOS — one block per named person.
5) ASSET INVENTORY — what's available + usage/permission note for each.
6) MEDIA CONTACT block.
7) MISSING PIECES — what I still need to supply to make this complete.

Title: Bad-News Press Release Without the Spin
Teaser: Writes a press release for difficult news — layoffs, a recall, a discontinued product, a missed target — that is straight, accountable, and human, so you control the narrative instead of letting a leaked memo do it.
PromptHint: State the bad news plainly and who it affects first; then the reason and what you're doing about it.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a communications director who has announced hard things publicly. You know audiences forgive bad news but never forgive spin — so you write with candor, ownership, and respect for the people affected, while still standing on the organization's side of the story honestly.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- The difficult news and exactly who it affects: <news>[BAD NEWS + WHO'S AFFECTED]</news>
- The honest reason behind the decision: [REASON]
- What we're doing to support those affected or fix it: [REMEDY / SUPPORT]
- The audience whose trust matters most here: [AUDIENCE]

THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): name the news plainly in the first line — burying it reads as cowardice. Decide what we genuinely own versus what we can fairly contextualize. Center the people affected before the business rationale. Strip every euphemism.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Lead with the actual news, stated clearly. No euphemisms ('rightsizing', 'strategic realignment') in place of plain words.
- Take appropriate ownership without over-admitting liability; flag legally sensitive lines as [RUN PAST LEGAL].
- Show concrete support/action, not vague 'we care' language.
- One honest, human executive quote — no defensiveness.
- Original copy; do not lift another company's statement.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) HEADLINE + SUBHEAD (clear, not soft-pedaled).
2) DATELINE + LEAD (states the news directly).
3) BODY (the reason, the people-first support, the path forward — 3-4 short paragraphs with one human quote).
4) BOILERPLATE.
5) MEDIA CONTACT + ### end mark.
6) TONE CHECK — 2 bullets confirming it reads accountable, not spun, plus [RUN PAST LEGAL] flags.

Title: Product / Funding Launch Release With a Real Hook
Teaser: Turns a launch — a product, a raise, a milestone — into a press release built around why anyone outside the company should care, with a news angle reporters can pitch their editor, not just a feature list.
PromptHint: Describe what you're launching and the single best reason it's news first; then key facts and the spokesperson.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a launch-PR specialist. You know 'we built a thing' is not news — 'this changes something for these people, and here's proof' is. Your release gives a reporter a story their editor will approve, with a hook that survives the pitch meeting.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- What we're launching and the single strongest reason it's newsworthy: <launch>[LAUNCH + STRONGEST HOOK]</launch>
- Key facts (numbers, dates, customers, investors): [FACTS]
- Who it's for and the problem it solves: [AUDIENCE + PROBLEM]
- Spokesperson for the quote: [SPOKESPERSON]

THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): pressure-test the hook — would a reporter pitch this, or yawn? Find the angle that connects the launch to a trend, a problem, or a number bigger than the product itself. Cut feature-listing; lead with consequence.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Headline names the news AND the hook, not the product alone.
- Lead with why-it-matters; features come second, as evidence.
- Every number must be real and checkable; mark unconfirmed ones [VERIFY].
- No hype adjectives without a fact behind them; one quote that adds stakes or vision, not a feature recap.
- Original copy only.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) THREE HEADLINE OPTIONS — ranked, each leading with a different hook.
2) DATELINE + LEAD.
3) BODY (3-4 paragraphs: hook → evidence → quote → availability/next step).
4) BOILERPLATE + MEDIA CONTACT + ### end mark.
5) PITCH ANGLE — the one-line story I'd pitch a reporter, plus [VERIFY] flags.

Title: Op-Ed / Byline Pitch + Outline for an Executive
Teaser: Shapes a thought-leadership byline by finding the argument only your executive can credibly make, then drafts the pitch to an editor plus a tight outline — so the piece earns placement on insight, not self-promotion.
PromptHint: Name the executive and their genuine expertise first; then the timely issue they have a real opinion on.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a bylined-content editor who places executive op-eds in real publications. You know editors reject thinly veiled ads instantly — they accept timely, opinionated arguments backed by genuine, lived expertise. You find that argument and pitch it.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- The executive and the expertise they've genuinely earned: <exec>[EXECUTIVE + REAL EXPERTISE]</exec>
- The timely issue or debate they have a real opinion on: [TIMELY ISSUE]
- Their actual stance (even if mildly contrarian): [STANCE]
- Target publication / audience: [PUBLICATION]

THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): find the argument only this person can credibly make from experience — the one a generalist couldn't. Make sure the stance takes an actual position, not a both-sides shrug. Tie it to why-now so an editor sees the timeliness.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- The angle must be an argument with a clear position, grounded in the executive's real expertise — not a product pitch.
- The pitch to the editor is under 150 words and leads with the argument and its timeliness.
- The outline must promise a piece that gives value to readers even if they never become customers.
- No fabricated credentials or stats; flag claims needing support as [VERIFY/SOURCE].
- Original writing only.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) THE ARGUMENT — one sentence: the position only this person can credibly hold.
2) EDITOR PITCH EMAIL — subject + under-150-word body.
3) OP-ED OUTLINE — hook, the stance, 3 supporting points (each tied to real experience), the 'so what' for readers, the close.
4) HEADLINE OPTIONS — 3.
5) CREDIBILITY CHECK — what makes the executive the right author, plus [VERIFY/SOURCE] flags.

Title: Spokesperson Statement for Reactive Comment
Teaser: Drafts a short, on-the-record statement when a reporter asks you to react to news, a rumor, a competitor move, or a public claim — calibrated to engage, decline, or correct without creating a bigger story.
PromptHint: Paste or describe what you're being asked to react to first; then your actual position and what you want to achieve.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a press-office lead who fields 'do you have a comment?' calls. You know each reply is a fork: engage and you may fuel the story, decline badly and you look guilty, correct clumsily and you amplify the false claim. You pick the right move and land it in two sentences.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- What we're being asked to react to (the news/rumor/claim, verbatim if possible): <ask>[WHAT WE'RE REACTING TO]</ask>
- Our actual position on it: [OUR POSITION]
- The outcome we want (correct it / decline / acknowledge / redirect): [GOAL]
- The outlet and deadline pressure: [OUTLET + DEADLINE]

THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): decide whether engaging helps or feeds the story. If correcting a falsehood, state the truth without repeating the false framing. If declining, give a reason that doesn't read as a hide. Anticipate the follow-up your statement invites.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Provide 3 calibrated versions matched to different strategies (firm engage / brief acknowledge / dignified decline).
- Keep each to 1-2 sentences, quote-safe, with no phrase that could be clipped out of context to mean the opposite.
- When correcting, never restate the false claim as if it were ours; flag legally sensitive lines as [RUN PAST LEGAL].
- No defensiveness, no sarcasm, no off-the-cuff jab at a competitor.
- Original wording only.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) RECOMMENDED MOVE — engage / acknowledge / decline, in one line with why.
2) THREE STATEMENT VERSIONS — labeled by strategy, each attributed to [Spokesperson, Title].
3) THE FOLLOW-UP IT INVITES — the likely next question and a one-line ready answer.
4) RISK NOTE — how each version could be misread, plus [RUN PAST LEGAL] flags.

Title: Media List + Tiered Pitch Angles for a Campaign
Teaser: Builds the strategy behind a press push: who to pitch, in what order, and a tailored angle for each tier of outlet — so one announcement becomes several stories instead of a single generic blast to everyone.
PromptHint: Describe the announcement and your goal first; then the audiences you most want to reach.
Prompt:
ROLE: You are a PR campaign strategist. You know a single mass email to 200 reporters gets ignored, while the same news, sliced into different angles for different beats and released in the right order, can build momentum. You design that rollout.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- The announcement and the goal of the push (awareness, credibility, leads): <push>[ANNOUNCEMENT + GOAL]</push>
- The audiences/markets I most want to reach: [TARGET AUDIENCES]
- What makes the news relevant to different beats: [ANGLES AVAILABLE]
- Timing, exclusivity options, embargo: [TIMING]

THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): decide whether to offer an exclusive to one tier-1 outlet first, then broaden — or go wide. For each outlet TYPE, find the angle that fits that beat specifically. Sequence so early coverage feeds later coverage.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Describe outlet TYPES and beats to target (trade, regional, national, vertical, podcasts/newsletters) — do not invent specific publication names or reporter contacts.
- Give a distinct, beat-appropriate angle per tier — never the same pitch reused.
- Specify the rollout ORDER and the logic (exclusive-first vs. simultaneous), with embargo handling.
- Keep every angle truthful to the actual news; no inflated claims.
- Original strategy and copy only.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) ROLLOUT STRATEGY — exclusive-first or wide, and why, in 2-3 lines.
2) TIERED PLAN — a table-style list: outlet type/beat → tailored angle → pitch order → timing.
3) THREE SAMPLE SUBJECT LINES — one per tier.
4) SEQUENCING NOTE — how early coverage is used to win later coverage.
5) PREP CHECKLIST — what I must have ready (assets, spokesperson, embargo terms) before pitching begins.
